  • Download Rmd

Phishing URL Detection — Hypothesis & EDA

K-o-m-A

2026-04-20

library(tidyverse)
library(corrplot)
library(patchwork)
library(scales)

1. Problem Statement

1.1 Real-world scenario

A user clicks a link in an email, chat, or search result. The browser or a corporate proxy has to decide before the page loads whether the link is phishing. The cheapest signal at that moment is the URL string itself — no DNS lookup, no HTML fetch, no rendering. If a URL-only check is good enough, we can block the link for free. If it isn’t, we have to fall back to a slower page-level check.

The question we want to answer is simple: how much phishing signal is already in the URL, and how few features do we really need? If a URL-only model works well, we can run it as a fast first-line filter. Where it fails, the slower trust- and behavior-level checks take over.

This lines up with our two chosen scenarios:

  • Scenario 2 — parametric vs. non-parametric models on the URL-only tier. Tells us whether a linear model is enough, or whether the URL hides non-linear patterns that only RF/SVM/GBM can pick up.
  • Scenario 3 — algorithmic vs. embedded feature selection on the full feature matrix. Tells us how few features we really need, and which ones survive penalisation when features are correlated.

1.2 Dataset

The PhiUSIIL dataset has ~235 k rows and 50 predictor features. We split them into three families by how expensive they are to compute:

Family What it captures Example features
Lexical Properties of the URL string URLLength, NoOfSubDomain, NoOfObfuscatedChar, CharContinuationRate
Trust Domain / title consistency IsHTTPS, HasTitle, HasFavicon, Bank, Pay, Crypto
Behavior Page-level content signals NoOfURLRedirect, NoOfiFrame, HasExternalFormSubmit, HasHiddenFields

2. Hypotheses

H1 (Scenario 2) — On the URL-only tier, non-parametric models pick up meaningfully more signal than parametric ones

Phishing URLs usually leave lexical traces: odd subdomain depth, paths full of digits, obfuscated characters. None of this needs the page to be downloaded. We expect a model trained only on Lexical features (URL string, no DNS, no HTML) to still reach a reasonable AUC — but with a bigger parametric vs. non-parametric gap than any other tier. The reason: lexical patterns interact in non-additive ways (lengths × digit counts × special chars), and a linear decision boundary can’t fully use those interactions. We compare three parametric methods (LR, LDA, Naive Bayes) against three non-parametric ones (RF, SVM-RBF, KNN).

Concrete success criteria. To keep the claim precise, H1 is tested against the 5-fold CV protocol from scenario_2.rmd:

# Criterion Threshold
C1 Mean CV AUC gap between the best non-parametric and best parametric model on the Lexical (URLOnly) tier ΔAUC ≥ 0.02
C2 Paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test on the 5 per-fold AUCs of those two models p < 0.05
C3 Gradient across tiers: the Lexical gap is strictly larger than the Full-tier gap (Full saturates at AUC ≈ 0.99 and the gap should drop to ≤ 0.005). gap(Lexical) > gap(Full)

H1 is supported only if C1 and C2 hold. C3 turns the claim from “non-parametric wins on one tier” into the stronger statement “the non-parametric advantage shrinks as the feature tier gets stronger.” H1 is rejected if either C1 or C2 fails on Lexical.

H2 (Scenario 3) — Embedded feature selection keeps clearly fewer features than algorithmic selection, because it collapses correlated clusters

The full feature matrix has severe multicollinearity (a URL-length cluster with VIF > 1000, see Section 4.3) and several pairs with |r| > 0.7. We expect embedded methods (LASSO, ElasticNet, Random Forest permutation importance) to keep fewer features than algorithmic methods (forward/backward stepwise, RFE). The reason: embedded penalisation pulls each correlated cluster down to one or two survivors, while stepwise search treats correlated features as independent candidates. We will report (a) how many features each method keeps, (b) how much the kept sets overlap, and (c) which features stop being statistically significant when their correlated partners are added or removed.

3. Data Loading & Cleaning

raw <- read_csv(
  "PhiUSIIL_Phishing_URL_Dataset.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

# Strip UTF-8 BOM that appears on the first column name
names(raw) <- map_chr(names(raw), ~ str_replace(.x, "^\ufeff", ""))

# glimpse(raw)
# Columns excluded from modelling (identifiers / free text)
ID_COLS <- c("FILENAME", "URL", "Domain", "TLD", "Title")

# Binary target as factor
df <- raw %>%
  mutate(label_bin = factor(label, levels = c(0, 1),
                            labels = c("Legitimate", "Phishing"))) %>%
  select(-all_of(ID_COLS))

# Feature matrix (numeric predictors only)
features <- df %>% select(-label, -label_bin)

cat("Observations:", nrow(df), "\n")
Observations: 235795 
cat("Predictors:  ", ncol(features), "\n")
Predictors:   50

Feature-family mapping

The three families (Lexical / Trust / Behavior) are our own taxonomy, not a property of the dataset. They reflect the cost of obtaining each signal at decision time: Lexical — derivable from the raw URL string alone (zero network cost). Trust — requires light page/domain inspection or an external lookup (DNS, TLD database, HTTPS handshake, title parsing). Behavior — requires fetching, parsing and partially rendering the page.

Note on IsHTTPS: although the “https://” scheme is part of the URL string, we place it in the Trust family. Modern browsers refuse plain HTTP for any sensitive interaction and warn the user prominently, so the scheme is already enforced by the trust layer of the browser rather than acting as a free lexical clue. Treating it as Lexical would let the URLOnly tier cheat off a binary that is, in practice, a deployment/trust signal.

family_map <- tribble(
  ~feature,                    ~family,
  "URLLength",                 "Lexical",
  "DomainLength",              "Lexical",
  "IsDomainIP",                "Lexical",
  "URLSimilarityIndex",        "Trust",
  "CharContinuationRate",      "Lexical",
  "TLDLegitimateProb",         "Trust",
  "URLCharProb",               "Trust",
  "TLDLength",                 "Lexical",
  "NoOfSubDomain",             "Lexical",
  "HasObfuscation",            "Lexical",
  "NoOfObfuscatedChar",        "Lexical",
  "ObfuscationRatio",          "Lexical",
  "NoOfLettersInURL",          "Lexical",
  "LetterRatioInURL",          "Lexical",
  "NoOfDegitsInURL",           "Lexical",
  "DegitRatioInURL",           "Lexical",
  "NoOfEqualsInURL",           "Lexical",
  "NoOfQMarkInURL",            "Lexical",
  "NoOfAmpersandInURL",        "Lexical",
  "NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL","Lexical",
  "SpacialCharRatioInURL",     "Lexical",
  "IsHTTPS",                   "Trust",
  "LineOfCode",                "Behavior",
  "LargestLineLength",         "Behavior",
  "HasTitle",                  "Trust",
  "DomainTitleMatchScore",     "Trust",
  "URLTitleMatchScore",        "Trust",
  "HasFavicon",                "Trust",
  "Robots",                    "Trust",
  "IsResponsive",              "Behavior",
  "NoOfURLRedirect",           "Behavior",
  "NoOfSelfRedirect",          "Behavior",
  "HasDescription",            "Behavior",
  "NoOfPopup",                 "Behavior",
  "NoOfiFrame",                "Behavior",
  "HasExternalFormSubmit",     "Behavior",
  "HasSocialNet",              "Behavior",
  "HasSubmitButton",           "Behavior",
  "HasHiddenFields",           "Behavior",
  "HasPasswordField",          "Behavior",
  "Bank",                      "Trust",
  "Pay",                       "Trust",
  "Crypto",                    "Trust",
  "HasCopyrightInfo",          "Behavior",
  "NoOfImage",                 "Behavior",
  "NoOfCSS",                   "Behavior",
  "NoOfJS",                    "Behavior",
  "NoOfSelfRef",               "Behavior",
  "NoOfEmptyRef",              "Behavior",
  "NoOfExternalRef",           "Behavior"
)

3.3 Feature Exclusion

Before the EDA we drop three groups of features that would bias the analysis or trivialise the modelling. All three decisions are based on what the feature actually means.

Category 1 — Computed probability / similarity scores

These are not raw URL measurements. They are outputs of other models or expert-written scoring functions that already encode phishing-detection logic. Keeping them would mean we are classifying another classifier’s output, not the URL itself.

SCORES <- c(
  "URLSimilarityIndex",   # similarity to known-phishing URL patterns
  "TLDLegitimateProb",    # P(TLD is legitimate) from an external lookup
  "URLCharProb",          # P(character sequence) from a language model
  "DomainTitleMatchScore",# computed domain↔title consistency score
  "URLTitleMatchScore"    # computed URL↔title consistency score
)
cat("Computed scores to remove:", paste(SCORES, collapse = ", "), "\n")
Computed scores to remove: URLSimilarityIndex, TLDLegitimateProb, URLCharProb, DomainTitleMatchScore, URLTitleMatchScore

Category 2 — Redundant binary indicator

HasObfuscation is literally (NoOfObfuscatedChar > 0). It holds no information that the count feature doesn’t already carry.

REDUNDANT_BINARY <- "HasObfuscation"
cat("Redundant binary to remove:", REDUNDANT_BINARY, "\n")
Redundant binary to remove: HasObfuscation

Category 3 — Redundant ratio features

Each ratio below equals count / URLLength, and both the count and URLLength are already in the dataset. Keeping the ratio gives us a perfect linear dependence — and no extra signal.

RATIOS <- c(
  "LetterRatioInURL",      # = NoOfLettersInURL   / URLLength
  "DegitRatioInURL",       # = NoOfDegitsInURL    / URLLength
  "ObfuscationRatio",      # = NoOfObfuscatedChar / URLLength
  "SpacialCharRatioInURL"  # = NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL / URLLength
)
cat("Linearly dependent ratios to remove:", paste(RATIOS, collapse = ", "), "\n")
Linearly dependent ratios to remove: LetterRatioInURL, DegitRatioInURL, ObfuscationRatio, SpacialCharRatioInURL 
EXCLUDE_COLS <- c(SCORES, REDUNDANT_BINARY, RATIOS)

df       <- df %>% select(-all_of(EXCLUDE_COLS))
features <- df %>% select(-label, -label_bin)

cat("\nFeatures after exclusion:", ncol(features), "\n")

Features after exclusion: 40 
cat("Removed:", paste(EXCLUDE_COLS, collapse = ", "), "\n")
Removed: URLSimilarityIndex, TLDLegitimateProb, URLCharProb, DomainTitleMatchScore, URLTitleMatchScore, HasObfuscation, LetterRatioInURL, DegitRatioInURL, ObfuscationRatio, SpacialCharRatioInURL

Update the family map to reflect the reduced feature set:

family_map <- family_map %>%
  filter(!feature %in% EXCLUDE_COLS)

family_map %>% count(family) %>% print()
# A tibble: 3 × 2
  family       n
  <chr>    <int>
1 Behavior    20
2 Lexical     13
3 Trust        7

3.4 Feature Tiers Used in Modelling

To test H1 we define three nested feature tiers. The modelling notebook reads these tier definitions directly from here, so both notebooks stay in sync.

tier_lexical  <- family_map %>% filter(family == "Lexical")  %>% pull(feature)
tier_trust    <- family_map %>% filter(family == "Trust")    %>% pull(feature)
tier_behavior <- family_map %>% filter(family == "Behavior") %>% pull(feature)
tier_full     <- c(tier_lexical, tier_trust, tier_behavior)

cat("URLOnly tier (Lexical, ",  length(tier_lexical),  " features)\n", sep = "")
URLOnly tier (Lexical, 13 features)
cat("Trust tier              (",  length(tier_trust),    " features)\n", sep = "")
Trust tier              (7 features)
cat("Behavior tier           (",  length(tier_behavior), " features)\n", sep = "")
Behavior tier           (20 features)
cat("Full tier               (",  length(tier_full),     " features)\n", sep = "")
Full tier               (40 features)

4. Exploratory Data Analysis

4.1 Data Overview

4.1.1 Class Balance

df %>%
  count(label_bin) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(label_bin, n, fill = label_bin)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(pct, 0.1)), vjust = -0.4, size = 4) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("steelblue", "firebrick")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, .1))) +
  labs(title = "Class distribution",
       x = NULL, y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

Takeaway: The dataset is roughly balanced, so we don’t need resampling or class-weighting. Accuracy works as a secondary metric next to AUC.

4.1.2 Missing Values

No missing values are present in any predictor (verified with colSums(is.na(features))).

4.2 H1 — Lexical features overlap between classes, so we’ll need non-linear models

H1 claims that a URL-only detector (Lexical features) will show a bigger parametric vs. non-parametric gap than any other tier. We don’t test non-linearity directly in the EDA. Instead we show that per-feature discriminative power is lowest in the Lexical family. When no single feature tells the classes apart, the model has to combine several features at once — and that’s where non-linear models have an edge.

4.2.1 Discriminative Power by Family

We measure discriminative power separately for continuous and binary features. The standardised mean difference (SMD) only makes sense for continuous features. For binary features the “pooled SD” collapses to a function of the class proportion, so SMD stops being comparable across features. For those we use Cramér’s V instead — the standard effect-size measure for a 2×2 contingency table, on the same 0–1 scale as |correlation|.

# --- Continuous features: standardised mean difference ----------------------
smd_df <- df %>%
  select(label, all_of(continuous_features)) %>%
  pivot_longer(-label, names_to = "feature", values_to = "value") %>%
  group_by(feature, label) %>%
  summarise(m = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE), s = sd(value, na.rm = TRUE),
            .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = label, values_from = c(m, s),
              names_glue = "{.value}_{label}") %>%
  mutate(
    pooled_sd = sqrt((s_0^2 + s_1^2) / 2),
    effect    = abs(m_1 - m_0) / (pooled_sd + 1e-9),
    metric    = "SMD (continuous)"
  ) %>%
  select(feature, effect, metric)

# --- Binary features: Cramer's V on a 2x2 contingency table -----------------
cramers_v <- function(x, y) {
  tab <- table(x, y)
  if (any(dim(tab) < 2)) return(NA_real_)
  chi2 <- suppressWarnings(chisq.test(tab, correct = FALSE)$statistic)
  n <- sum(tab)
  as.numeric(sqrt(chi2 / (n * (min(dim(tab)) - 1))))
}

cv_df <- tibble(
  feature = binary_features,
  effect  = map_dbl(binary_features, ~ cramers_v(features[[.x]], df$label)),
  metric  = "Cramer's V (binary)"
)

discrim_df <- bind_rows(smd_df, cv_df) %>%
  left_join(family_map, by = "feature") %>%
  replace_na(list(family = "Other")) %>%
  arrange(desc(effect))

ggplot(discrim_df, aes(x = reorder(feature, effect), y = effect, fill = family)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  facet_wrap(~ metric, scales = "free", ncol = 2) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Per-feature discriminative power",
       subtitle = "Continuous: |SMD|. Binary: Cramer's V. Both >= 0.",
       x = NULL, y = "Effect size", fill = "Family") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

Effect-size distribution per family (continuous features only, so the SMD values are directly comparable):

smd_family <- discrim_df %>%
  filter(metric == "SMD (continuous)", family != "Other")

ggplot(smd_family, aes(family, effect, fill = family)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.5, size = 2) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "SMD distribution by family (continuous features only)",
       subtitle = "H1: Lexical SMD lowest -> largest parametric gap expected at URLOnly tier",
       x = NULL, y = "| SMD |") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

Takeaway (H1): Among the continuous features, Lexical has the lowest median |SMD| and the smallest spread. Trust and Behavior carry more per-feature signal. Weak per-feature evidence plus visible non-linear structure is exactly the setting where parametric models struggle most — and where the parametric vs. non-parametric gap predicted by H1 should show up.

4.3 H2 (Scenario 3) — Multicollinearity is what makes algorithmic vs. embedded selection differ

H2 says that algorithmic and embedded feature selection methods will keep different feature sets on the Full tier. The EDA evidence is the correlation structure: where are the correlated clusters, and how bad is the inflation? Embedded methods (LASSO, ElasticNet, RF importance) collapse each cluster down to one survivor. Algorithmic methods (stepwise, RFE) treat correlated features as independent candidates and tend to keep more of them.

4.3.1 Lexical Correlation Matrix

The collinearity that drives H2 lives inside the Lexical family. Trust and Behavior have mean_abs_r around 0.18 and 0.14 and no dense cluster. The plot below makes the URL-length count cluster visible.

lexical_cor <- features %>%
  select(all_of(tier_lexical)) %>%
  cor(use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

  corrplot(
  lexical_cor,
  method  = "color",
  order   = "hclust",
  tl.cex  = 0.7,
  tl.col  = "black",
  col     = colorRampPalette(c("firebrick", "white", "steelblue"))(200),
  title   = "Correlation - Lexical features",
  mar     = c(0, 0, 1.5, 0),
  addCoef.col = "black",
  number.cex  = 0.55
)

Across the full feature matrix, 10 pairs pass |r| > 0.7 — and all of them sit inside this Lexical count cluster (URLLength ↔︎ NoOfLettersInURL ↔︎ NoOfDegitsInURL and friends). These are the correlated pairs we expect algorithmic and embedded selection to handle differently.

4.3.2 Variance Inflation Factors

library(knitr)

# Target-free VIF from the design matrix (avoids non-converged GLM).
X_vif <- features %>%
  select(all_of(continuous_features)) %>%
  as.data.frame()

vif_values <- vapply(seq_along(X_vif), function(i) {
  r2 <- summary(lm(X_vif[[i]] ~ ., data = X_vif[, -i, drop = FALSE]))$r.squared
  1 / (1 - r2)
}, numeric(1))
names(vif_values) <- names(X_vif)

vif_df <- tibble(
  feature = names(vif_values),
  vif     = as.numeric(vif_values)
) %>%
  left_join(family_map, by = "feature") %>%
  replace_na(list(family = "Other")) %>%
  mutate(severity = case_when(
    vif > 10            ~ "severe (> 10)",
    vif >= 5 & vif <= 10 ~ "moderate (5-10)",
    TRUE                ~ "ok (< 5)"
  )) %>%
  arrange(desc(vif))

vif_df %>%
  transmute(Feature = feature,
            Family  = family,
            VIF     = round(vif, 2),
            Severity = severity) %>%
  kable(caption = "Variance Inflation Factors - continuous features (lm-based, target-free).")
Variance Inflation Factors - continuous features (lm-based, target-free).
Feature Family VIF Severity
URLLength Lexical 1070.20 severe (> 10)
NoOfLettersInURL Lexical 535.40 severe (> 10)
NoOfDegitsInURL Lexical 90.60 severe (> 10)
NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL Lexical 10.16 severe (> 10)
NoOfEqualsInURL Lexical 7.94 moderate (5-10)
NoOfObfuscatedChar Lexical 4.90 ok (< 5)
NoOfAmpersandInURL Lexical 3.18 ok (< 5)
CharContinuationRate Lexical 2.77 ok (< 5)
NoOfSelfRef Behavior 2.19 ok (< 5)
NoOfExternalRef Behavior 1.98 ok (< 5)
NoOfSubDomain Lexical 1.95 ok (< 5)
NoOfQMarkInURL Lexical 1.86 ok (< 5)
DomainLength Lexical 1.80 ok (< 5)
LineOfCode Behavior 1.38 ok (< 5)
NoOfImage Behavior 1.34 ok (< 5)
NoOfJS Behavior 1.13 ok (< 5)
NoOfiFrame Behavior 1.11 ok (< 5)
TLDLength Lexical 1.11 ok (< 5)
NoOfCSS Behavior 1.08 ok (< 5)
NoOfEmptyRef Behavior 1.03 ok (< 5)
LargestLineLength Behavior 1.01 ok (< 5)
NoOfPopup Behavior 1.01 ok (< 5)

Takeaway (H2): Dropping the four ratio features in Section 3.3 was necessary but not enough. URLLength, NoOfLettersInURL and NoOfDegitsInURL stay near-collinear because they measure overlapping properties of the same URL string. Stepwise/RFE will likely keep most of them; LASSO and RF importance should keep only one or two. Measuring that difference is the H2 task. Binary features are left out of the VIF calculation by design — regressing a 0/1 indicator on continuous predictors is not comparable on the same scale.

4.4 Skewness Assessment (Scenario 2 preparation)

# Skewness for all continuous features
skewness_fn <- function(x) {
  x <- x[!is.na(x)]
  n <- length(x)
  m <- mean(x)
  s <- sd(x)
  (n / ((n-1)*(n-2))) * sum(((x - m) / s)^3)
}

skew_df <- tibble(
  feature  = continuous_features,
  skewness = map_dbl(continuous_features, ~ skewness_fn(features[[.x]]))
) %>%
  left_join(family_map, by = "feature") %>%
  replace_na(list(family = "Other")) %>%
  mutate(abs_skew = abs(skewness)) %>%
  arrange(desc(abs_skew))

ggplot(skew_df, aes(x = reorder(feature, abs_skew), y = skewness, fill = family)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-2, 2), linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Skewness of continuous features",
       subtitle = "Dashed lines at +/- 2 mark heavy skew; LR/LDA may struggle here",
       x = NULL, y = "Skewness", fill = "Family") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

# Count features with |skewness| > 2
n_heavy_skew <- sum(skew_df$abs_skew > 2)
cat("\nFeatures with |skewness| > 2:", n_heavy_skew, "of",
    length(continuous_features), "\n")

Features with |skewness| > 2: 19 of 22 
cat("These are:\n")
These are:
skew_df %>% filter(abs_skew > 2) %>% select(feature, skewness, family) %>% print()
# A tibble: 19 × 3
   feature                    skewness family  
   <chr>                         <dbl> <chr>   
 1 NoOfCSS                      464.   Behavior
 2 NoOfObfuscatedChar           205.   Lexical 
 3 NoOfJS                       140.   Behavior
 4 NoOfEqualsInURL              115.   Lexical 
 5 NoOfEmptyRef                 107.   Behavior
 6 NoOfAmpersandInURL           107.   Lexical 
 7 NoOfiFrame                    97.7  Behavior
 8 NoOfDegitsInURL               94.9  Lexical 
 9 NoOfPopup                     85.0  Behavior
10 NoOfExternalRef               65.9  Behavior
11 NoOfSelfRef                   60.4  Behavior
12 NoOfLettersInURL              58.4  Lexical 
13 URLLength                     53.3  Lexical 
14 LineOfCode                    53.1  Behavior
15 LargestLineLength             48.2  Behavior
16 NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL    47.4  Lexical 
17 NoOfImage                     28.2  Behavior
18 NoOfQMarkInURL                 8.16 Lexical 
19 DomainLength                   2.51 Lexical

Takeaway (Scenario 2): Several count features are heavily right-skewed with long outlier tails. This shapes our modelling choices:

  • Parametric models (LR, LDA): need scaling and a log-transform on skewed features to make the distributional assumption hold.
  • Non-parametric models (trees, KNN, SVM): robust to skew, but KNN and SVM still need standardisation because they use distances.

5. EDA Summary & Modelling Implications

A note on separability: After the Section 3.3 exclusions, 40 features remain (13 Lexical + 7 Trust + 20 Behavior). Even on this reduced set, every model type already reaches AUC ≈ 0.99 on the Full tier — the task is near-perfectly separable once all three families are present. So we test H1 mainly on the URLOnly tier (13 Lexical features), where the parametric vs. non-parametric contrast has room to show. H2 is tested on the Full tier, where the multicollinearity that drives the algorithmic-vs-embedded gap actually lives.

5.1 Implications for modelling scenarios

Finding Scenario 2 implication (H1) Scenario 3 implication (H2)
Near-perfect separability on Full tier Comparison focused on URLOnly tier where models actually differ Use Full tier; selection methods need a non-trivial pool of redundant features
Lexical SMD lowest + features must be combined Largest parametric/non-parametric AUC gap expected on URLOnly Algorithmic methods may keep all weak Lexical features; embedded penalisation should drop them
Heavy skewness in 19 of 22 continuous features Log-transform + standardisation for LR/LDA; trees/RF unaffected Apply same transform before LASSO/ElasticNet (they are scale-sensitive)
URL-length cluster: VIF > 500 and abs(r) > 0.84 Use stable solvers / regularised LR for fairness Strongest test case for H2 — embedded should collapse cluster, stepwise should not
Trust binary features: low intra-correlation No special treatment All three Trust HTTPS-style flags likely retained by both selection families — informative-feature overlap
